about.quote1Tooltip: It's a Japanese proverb. Nana korobi ya oki. Every spider knows the drill. The web breaks, you rebuild. The web breaks again, you rebuild better. That's not persistence, that's an origin story on repeat.
about.quote2: With great power comes great responsibility.
about.quote2Tooltip: The quote that every Spider knows by heart. Uncle Ben said it once and the entire multiverse has been unpacking it ever since. It's not really about having power. It's about what you owe the world the moment you realize you do.
about.quote3: With great ability comes great accountability.
about.quote3Tooltip: Miles' pops' version hits different because he's not a superhero. He's a cop and a father. Ability isn't a gift you stumble into. And accountability isn't just owning the wins. It's standing in front of the outcome, whatever it is, and saying 'that was me.'
about.quote4: With great power comes great respect.
about.quote4Tooltip: Norman didn't misquote Uncle Ben. He corrected him. Power isn't a burden you carry for others. It's leverage. Respect, the way he means it, isn't earned through sacrifice. It's commanded. The scary part isn't that he's wrong. It's that the world keeps proving him right.
about.quote5: With no power comes no responsibility.
about.quote5Tooltip: New York's first and only superhero, now a washed-up PI who hung up the mask years ago. When 'The Spider' says this, he ain't joking. It's what a man tells himself when he's tired of saving a city that never saved him back.
about.quote6Tooltip: The man who started it all didn't just create Spider-Man. He created the idea that Spider-Man could be anyone. Not a power fantasy for the chosen few. A mirror for the rest of us.
about.loreDesignation: Web-Designer, Web-Slinger. Tomato, tomato. Wait, y'all are still on Web2? Oh, it's ghetto over here.
about.loreOrigin: I borrowed some tech from Kang No. 1337, and we don't have a designation for whatever Earth you're reading this from. Don't tell him though.
about.loreMotto: Something my folks told me long before I became a spida, so I etched it under my visor as a reminder.
about.originBody1p1: My dad coached me at my first competitive gaming tournament. Halo, for the Xbox, back when Halo 2 was about to drop. I came in third and was hard on myself about it. He still picked me up after school with the Limited Edition and the Master Chief helmet. I'll never forget how big of a smile he had on his face.
about.originBody1p2: He's also the one who handed me my first Spider-Man comic. Everything else came from that.
about.originBody1p3: That's what pop culture was in my house. It wasn't entertainment. It was how my dad and I talked. He had his baseball cards and Magic: The Gathering collection. I had my Pokémon and Yu-Gi-Oh! decks. We'd spend hours comparing pulls, debating strategies, nerding out over whatever we were into that week. It wasn't about the cards. It was about having a language that was ours.
about.originBody1p4: Every Spider-Person has a canon event, something that shapes who they become. This was mine. The moment I realized that the things we love aren't just things. They're how we find each other. And some of the strongest ways to stick together.
about.originBody2p1: I lost my father to the pandemic. And in the years since, aunts, uncles, friends. People who shaped me in ways I'm still figuring out.
about.originBody2p2: Every Spider-Person loses someone. Uncle Ben. Gwen Stacy. Aunt May. It's supposed to be the thing that forges you, the event the multiverse says you have to go through to become who you're meant to be. But that's only half the story. Peter didn't just lose people. He held on harder to the ones still here. Miles. MJ. Even the ones who drive him crazy. The webs he built weren't just for swinging. They were for holding things together.
about.originBody2p3: In Across the Spider-Verse, Miles tries everything to stop his father's death from becoming a canon event. And Peter B. Parker is learning how to be a dad for the first time, terrified he's going to mess it up. I felt both of those at the same time. The kid who'd do anything to keep his father around, and the man who isn't sure he's built for the role himself.
about.originBody2p4: When things got heavy, I kept coming back to that. Not because Spider-Man had answers, but because he never stopped showing up. For the city. For the people he loved. For strangers he'd never meet again. Fall down 7, get up 8. The people I've lost are part of why I keep getting back up. The people still here are why it matters.
about.originTitle3: Canon Event #003: The Leap of Faith
about.originBody3p1: Knowing why it matters is one thing. Deciding what to do about it is another.
about.originBody3p2: There's a moment in Into the Spider-Verse where the multiverse tells Miles there are things he has to accept. Events he can't change. A script he's supposed to follow. And he says no. Not because he has all the answers, but because he decides that who he becomes is his call to make.
about.originBody3p3: I wish I'd taken more leaps when my pops was around. I wanted to make him as proud of me as I am to have had him as a dad. But the thing about a leap of faith is that you don't get to pick the timing. You just have to be honest about when you're ready. Not confident. Not fearless. Just ready.
about.originBody3p4: This is that jump. Not perfect, not following anyone else's blueprint. Built from the things I love, the people who shaped me, and the belief that none of it was ever just for me. Pop culture gave me a language. Loss gave me a reason. And everyone still in my corner gave me the nerve.
about.originBody3p5: "Anyone can wear the mask." Stan Lee said that. It means Spider-Man's world doesn't belong to people who love the mask but hate the face underneath it. Miles is Spider-Man. Always was. If that bothers you, this isn't your web. But if you're kind, passionate, and open-minded? If you believe these stories belong to everyone who genuinely loves them? Welcome. It's yours too.
about.originBody4p1: Now that you're here, you should know how the web works.
about.originBody4p2: Your reactions are anonymous. You can react to any article on the site. We don't track who clicked what. No accounts, no profiles, no data harvesting. You felt something, you left a mark. That's it.
about.originBody4p3: Sharing is clean. Share links don't carry tracking parameters. No UTM codes, no referral IDs. Just the article. If you're putting someone onto something, the thing itself should be enough.
about.originBody4p4: There's no comments section. That's intentional. The web isn't a debate stage. If something moves you, the reactions and share buttons are there. If you want to talk about it, that's what the Discord is for.
about.originBody4p5: The gallery credits every artist. Every piece links back to the original creator. This site doesn't repost without attribution. Ever.
about.originBody4p6: The site doesn't sell anything. No merch store, no affiliate links, no sponsored content. The Spidaverse exists because it wants to, not because it has to.
about.originBody4p7: That's the web. Simple, clean, and built for people who actually care about this stuff. Swing free.
about.skillCommunityTagline: The web needs people in it
about.gadgetCategoryBadgesTagline: Tagging system for the multiverse catalog
about.webRatingDescription: Every piece of media gets a Web Rating out of 100, visualized as a spider web that fills based on the score. Not because I want to debate whether something deserves an 87 or a 91, but because sometimes you need to put a number on how much something mattered to you.
about.beliefText1: I write like I'm putting a friend onto something. Honest, but never just to be loud.
about.beliefText2: Not everything needs to be a masterpiece to be worth your time. Some things are comfort, some things challenge you. I try to meet all of it where it is.
about.beliefText3: This isn't a one-person operation. If something resonates, if you disagree, if you want to put me onto something I missed, that's the whole point.
